facts.text: Franklin wrote his autobiography as a letter to his son William Franklin.
Franklin began writing his autobiography in 1771 while visiting Bishop Shipley at Twyford in Hampshire, England.
Franklin completed the first part of his autobiography by 1731.
Franklin returned to Philadelphia with his manuscript in 1775.
Franklin left his manuscript in Philadelphia when he went to France in 1776.
Franklin resumed work on his autobiography in 1784 at Passy near Paris.
Franklin continued working on his autobiography in 1788 when he returned to Philadelphia.
Franklin stopped writing his autobiography when the narrative reached 1757.
Franklin was old, infirm, and suffering when he resumed work on his autobiography in 1788.
Franklin was still engaged in public service while working on his autobiography in 1788.
The first edition of Franklin's autobiography was published in French at Paris in 1791.
Franklin designated his grandson William Temple Franklin as his literary executor.
Franklin left his manuscript to his grandson William Temple Franklin.
Franklin had ever had pleasure in obtaining anecdotes of his ancestors.
Franklin made inquiries among his relations when in England.
Franklin undertook a journey to learn about his ancestors.
Franklin emerged from poverty and obscurity to affluence and reputation.
Franklin experienced a considerable share of felicity through his life.
Franklin believed his posterity would want to know the means by which he succeeded.
Franklin attributed his success to the blessing of God.
Franklin wrote his autobiography at Twyford at the Bishop of St. Asaph's in 1771.
Franklin emerged from poverty and obscurity to a state of affluence and reputation.
Franklin experienced considerable felicity throughout his life.
Franklin would have chosen to live his life over again if given the choice.
Franklin believed vanity is often productive of good to the possessor and others.
Franklin attributed his happiness to God's kind providence.
Franklin's family lived in Ecton, Northamptonshire for three hundred years.
Franklin's family was engaged in the smith's business for generations.
Franklin was the youngest son of the youngest son for five generations back.
Franklin's grandfather Thomas was born in 1598.
Franklin's grandfather Thomas lived with his son John, a dyer at Banbury.
Franklin saw his grandfather's gravestone in 1758.
Franklin's great-uncle Thomas was bred a smith but became a scrivener.
Franklin's great-uncle Thomas died on January 6, 1702, four years to the day before Franklin was born.
Franklin's uncle John was bred a dyer of woollens.
Franklin's uncle Benjamin was bred a silk dyer and served an apprenticeship at London.
Franklin's uncle Benjamin came to Boston and lived in Franklin's father's house for some years.
Franklin was named after his uncle Benjamin.
Franklin's uncle Benjamin taught him short-hand, which Franklin later forgot.
Franklin's uncle Benjamin was very pious and a great attender of sermons.
Franklin's uncle Benjamin was much of a politician.
Franklin's family was early in the Reformation and remained Protestant through Queen Mary's reign.
Franklin's family hid an English Bible under a joint-stool during Queen Mary's reign.
Franklin's father Josiah married young and carried his wife with three children to New England around 1682.
Franklin's father Josiah had seventeen children total by two wives.
Franklin's father Josiah had thirteen children who all grew up to be men and women and married.
Franklin was the youngest son and the youngest child but two.
Franklin was born in Boston, New England.
Franklin's mother was Abiah Folger, daughter of Peter Folger.
Franklin's maternal grandfather Peter Folger was one of the first settlers of New England.
Franklin's maternal grandfather Peter Folger wrote a piece in 1675 in favor of liberty of conscience.
Franklin's elder brothers were all put apprentices to different trades.
Franklin was put to grammar-school at eight years of age.
Franklin's father intended to devote him to the service of the Church.
Franklin showed early readiness in learning to read.
Franklin rose from the middle of his grammar-school class to the head of it in less than a year.
Franklin's father removed him from grammar-school due to the expense of college education.
Franklin attended a school for writing and arithmetic kept by Mr. George Brownell.
Franklin acquired fair writing under Mr. Brownell but failed in arithmetic.
Franklin was taken home at ten years old to assist his father in the tallow-chandler and soap-boiler business.
Franklin's father had assumed the tallow-chandler business after his dyeing trade failed in New England.
Franklin disliked the tallow-chandler trade.
Franklin had a strong inclination for the sea, but his father declared against it.
Franklin lived near water and learned early to swim well.
Franklin learned to manage boats.
Franklin was commonly allowed to govern boats and was generally a leader among boys.
Franklin sometimes led boys into scrapes.
Franklin proposed building a wharf on a salt-marsh near the mill-pond.
Franklin and his playfellows stole stones intended for a new house to build their wharf.
Franklin and his companions were discovered and corrected by their fathers.
Franklin's father convinced him that nothing was useful which was not honest.
Franklin's father had an excellent constitution of body and was of middle stature.
Franklin's father was ingenious and could draw prettily.
Franklin's father was skilled in music and had a clear, pleasing voice.
Franklin's father played psalm tunes on his violin and sang.
Franklin and his playfellows stole stones from a construction site to build a wharf.
Franklin's father corrected him for stealing the stones and taught him that nothing useful can be dishonest.
Franklin's father was of middle stature, well set, and very strong.
Franklin's father had a mechanical genius and was handy with tradesmen's tools.
Franklin's father excelled in sound understanding and solid judgment in prudential matters.
Franklin's father was frequently consulted by leading people about town and church affairs.
Franklin's father was often chosen as an arbitrator between contending parties.
Franklin's father liked to have sensible friends or neighbors at his table for conversation.
Franklin's father deliberately started ingenious or useful topics of discourse at meals to improve his children's minds.
Franklin's father paid little attention to the quality or flavor of food at meals.
Franklin was brought up to be indifferent to what kind of food was set before him.
Franklin cannot remember what he ate a few hours after dinner.
Franklin's mother had an excellent constitution and nursed all ten of her children.
Franklin's father lived to age 89 without serious illness.
Franklin's mother lived to age 85 without serious illness.
Franklin's parents were buried together at Boston.
Franklin's parents lived together in wedlock for fifty-five years.
Franklin's parents maintained a large family comfortably without an estate or gainful employment.
Franklin's parents brought up thirteen children and seven grandchildren.
Franklin's father was born in 1655 and died in 1744.
Franklin's mother was born in 1667 and died in 1752.
Franklin continued in his father's tallow-chandling business for two years until age twelve.
Franklin disliked the tallow-chandling trade.
Franklin's father feared Franklin would run away to sea like his son Josiah had done.
Franklin's father took him to observe joiners, bricklayers, turners, and braziers at work.
Franklin developed a lasting pleasure in watching good workmen handle their tools.
Franklin learned enough from observing tradesmen to do small jobs in his house and construct machines for experiments.
Franklin's father initially fixed upon the cutler's trade for him.
Franklin was sent to apprentice with his uncle Benjamin's son Samuel, a cutler in Boston.
Franklin was taken home from the cutler's apprenticeship because his uncle expected a fee.
Franklin was fond of reading from childhood.
Franklin spent all his small money on books.
Franklin's first book collection consisted of John Bunyan's works in separate little volumes.
Franklin sold Bunyan's works to buy R. Burton's Historical Collections.
Franklin's father's library consisted chiefly of books in polemic divinity.
Franklin read abundantly from Plutarch's Lives.
Franklin read DeFoe's Essay on Projects.
Franklin read Dr. Mather's Essays to do Good.
Franklin believed Essays to do Good influenced some principal future events of his life.
Franklin's bookish inclination determined his father to make him a printer.
Franklin's brother James was already a printer.
Franklin's brother James returned from England in 1717 with a press and letters.
Franklin liked printing much better than his father's trade.
Franklin still had a hankering for the sea.
Franklin was bound as an apprentice to his brother at age twelve.
Franklin was to serve as an apprentice until age twenty-one, with journeyman's wages in the last year.
Franklin made great proficiency in printing and became a useful hand to his brother.
Franklin had access to better books through his apprenticeship.
Franklin borrowed books from apprentices of booksellers and returned them promptly and clean.
Franklin often sat up reading most of the night when he had borrowed a book.
Matthew Adams, an ingenious tradesman, noticed Franklin and invited him to his library.
Matthew Adams kindly lent Franklin books of his choice.
Franklin took a fancy to poetry and made some little pieces.
Franklin's brother encouraged him to compose occasional ballads.
Franklin wrote a ballad called The Lighthouse Tragedy about the drowning of Captain Worthilake and his two daughters.
Franklin wrote a sailor's song about the taking of Teach (Blackbeard) the pirate.
Franklin's first ballad sold wonderfully because the event was recent.
Franklin's father discouraged his poetry by ridiculing his performances and saying verse-makers were generally beggars.
Franklin escaped being a poet, probably a very bad one.
Franklin found prose writing to be of great use in his life and a principal means of his advancement.
Franklin was intimately acquainted with John Collins, a bookish lad in town.
Franklin and Collins frequently disputed and were fond of argument.
Franklin had caught a disputatious habit from reading his father's books of religious dispute.
Franklin and Collins debated the propriety of educating the female sex in learning.
Franklin took the contrary side to Collins, perhaps partly for dispute's sake.
Collins was naturally more eloquent and had a ready plenty of words.
Franklin had the advantage of correct spelling and pointing due to the printing-house.
Franklin's father found his papers and critiqued his manner of writing.
Franklin's father told him he fell short in elegance of expression, method, and perspicuity.
Franklin determined to endeavor at improvement in his writing after his father's critique.
Franklin met with an odd volume of the Spectator, the third volume.
Franklin thought the Spectator's writing was excellent and wished to imitate it.
Franklin took papers from the Spectator, made short hints of sentiments, and tried to rewrite them from memory.
Franklin compared his rewritten Spectator papers with the originals and corrected his faults.
Franklin found he wanted a stock of words and readiness in recollecting them.
Franklin turned some Spectator tales into verse and later back into prose.
Franklin jumbled his collections of hints and tried to reduce them into the best order.
Franklin used this method to teach himself method in the arrangement of thoughts.
Franklin sometimes fancied he had improved the method of the language in certain particulars.
Franklin was extremely ambitious to become a tolerable English writer.
Franklin did his writing exercises and reading at night after work, before work began, or on Sundays.
Franklin contrived to be in the printing-house alone on Sundays to avoid public worship.
Franklin evaded common attendance on public worship which his father used to exact.
Franklin still thought public worship was a duty but could not afford time to practice it.
Franklin was about sixteen years old when he met with a book by Tryon recommending a vegetable diet.
Franklin determined to adopt a vegetable diet.
Franklin's brother boarded himself and his apprentices in another family.
Franklin's refusal to eat flesh occasioned inconvenience and he was frequently chid for his singularity.
Franklin learned Tryon's manner of preparing vegetable dishes such as boiling potatoes or rice and making hasty pudding.
Franklin proposed to his brother that he board himself for half the money paid for his board.
Franklin's brother instantly agreed to this arrangement.
Franklin found he could save half what his brother paid him for board.
Franklin used his savings as an additional fund for buying books.
Franklin remained alone in the printing-house while his brother and others went to meals.
Franklin's light repasts often consisted of no more than a biscuit, slice of bread, handful of raisins, a tart, and water.
Franklin made greater progress in study due to the time saved and the clearness of head from temperance.
Franklin was once made ashamed of his ignorance in figures.
Franklin had twice failed in learning figures when at school.
Franklin took Cocker's book of Arithmetic and went through it by himself with great ease.
Franklin read Seller's and Shermy's books of Navigation.
Franklin became acquainted with the little geometry contained in navigation books.
Franklin never proceeded far in geometry.
Franklin read Locke's On Human Understanding.
Franklin read the Art of Thinking by Messrs. du Port Royal.
Franklin met with an English grammar with sketches of rhetoric and logic at the end.
Franklin procured Xenophon's Memorable Things of Socrates.
Franklin was charmed with the Socratic method of dispute.
Franklin adopted the Socratic method and dropped his abrupt contradiction and positive argumentation.
Franklin put on the manner of a humble inquirer and doubter.
Franklin became a real doubter in many points of religious doctrine from reading Shaftesbury and Collins.
Franklin read Locke's 'On Human Understanding' during his youth.
Franklin read 'The Art of Thinking' by the scholars of Port Royal.
Franklin studied an English grammar book, possibly Greenwood's, that included sketches of rhetoric and logic.
Franklin read Xenophon's 'Memorable Things of Socrates'.
Franklin was charmed by the Socratic method of argumentation.
Franklin adopted the Socratic method and abandoned abrupt contradiction and positive argumentation.
Franklin became a doubter in many points of religious doctrine after reading Shaftesbury and Collins.
Franklin practiced the Socratic method continually and became very skilled at it.
Franklin used the Socratic method to draw people into concessions and entangle them in difficulties.
Franklin gradually abandoned the Socratic method after a few years.
Franklin developed a habit of expressing himself with modest diffidence.
Franklin avoided using words like 'certainly' and 'undoubtedly' when advancing opinions.
Franklin believed his habit of modest diffidence gave him great advantage in persuading others.
Franklin believed the chief ends of conversation are to inform, be informed, please, or persuade.
Franklin believed a positive, assuming manner tends to create opposition and defeat the purposes of speech.
Franklin's brother James began printing a newspaper in 1720 or 1721.
Franklin's brother's newspaper, the New England Courant, was the second newspaper to appear in America.
Franklin was employed to carry the New England Courant papers through the streets to customers.
Franklin wrote anonymous papers for his brother's newspaper while still a boy.
Franklin disguised his handwriting when submitting papers to his brother's newspaper.
Franklin's anonymous papers were approved by his brother's writing friends.
Franklin kept his authorship secret until his fund of ideas was exhausted.
Franklin's discovery as the author made his brother displeased, thinking it made Franklin too vain.
Franklin's brother considered himself Franklin's master and Franklin his apprentice.
Franklin thought his brother demean'd him too much in the services required.
Franklin and his brother's disputes were often brought before their father.
Franklin was generally favored in judgments by his father over his brother.
Franklin's brother was passionate and often beat Franklin.
Franklin resented his brother's beatings greatly.
Franklin found his apprenticeship very tedious.
A piece in the New England Courant on a political point gave offense to the Assembly.
Franklin's brother was taken up, censured, and imprisoned for a month for not disclosing the author of an offensive piece.
Franklin was taken up and examined before the council but was admonished and dismissed.
During his brother's confinement, Franklin managed the newspaper.
Franklin gave the rulers some critical remarks in the newspaper during his brother's confinement.
Franklin began to be considered by others as a young genius with a turn for libeling and satire.
The House issued an order that James Franklin should no longer print the New England Courant.
Franklin's brother arranged for the paper to be printed under Benjamin Franklin's name to evade the order.
Franklin signed new private indentures to secure his brother's benefit of his service.
Franklin later asserted his freedom, presuming his brother would not produce the new indentures.
Franklin considered this assertion of freedom one of the first errors of his life.
Franklin resented his brother's passionate blows more than the unfairness of his own action.
Franklin's brother prevented him from getting employment in any other printing-house in Boston.
Franklin decided to go to New York as the nearest place with a printer.
Franklin was inclined to leave Boston because he had made himself obnoxious to the governing party.
Franklin feared the arbitrary proceedings of the Assembly might bring him into scrapes if he stayed.
Franklin's indiscreet disputations about religion made him pointed at with horror as an infidel or atheist.
Franklin's father sided with his brother when Franklin wanted to leave.
Franklin's friend Collins arranged his passage to New York on a sloop.
Franklin sold some of his books to raise money for his passage.
Franklin left Boston at age 17 without recommendation or knowledge of anyone in New York.
Franklin had very little money in his pocket when he arrived in New York.
Franklin's inclinations for the sea had worn out by the time he reached New York.
Franklin offered his service to William Bradford, the printer in New York.
William Bradford told Franklin that his son in Philadelphia might employ him.
Franklin set out in a boat for Amboy, leaving his chest to follow by sea.
A squall tore the boat's rotten sails to pieces and drove them upon Long Island.
Franklin rescued a drunken Dutchman who fell overboard.
The Dutchman had a Dutch copy of Bunyan's Pilgrim's Progress, which Franklin dried for him.
Franklin considered Bunyan's Pilgrim's Progress his old favorite author.
Franklin believed Bunyan's Pilgrim's Progress has been more generally read than any book except perhaps the Bible.
Franklin admired Bunyan's method of mixing narration and dialogue.
Franklin noted that De Foe imitated Bunyan's method in his works.
Franklin noted that Richardson imitated Bunyan's method in his Pamela.
Franklin and the boatman spent thirty hours on the water without victuals or drink except filthy rum.
Franklin developed a fever after the boat journey.
Franklin treated his fever by drinking cold water plentifully, following a prescription he had read.
Franklin's fever left him after sweating plentifully through the night.
Franklin proceeded on foot from Amboy toward Philadelphia.
Franklin had fifty miles to travel to Burlington.
It rained very hard during Franklin's journey, soaking him thoroughly.
Franklin was suspected of being a runaway servant during his journey.
Franklin stopped at a poor inn and wished he had never left home.
Franklin met Dr. Brown at an inn within eight or ten miles of Burlington.
Dr. Brown became very sociable and friendly with Franklin after learning he had read a little.
Franklin's acquaintance with Dr. Brown continued as long as Dr. Brown lived.
Dr. Brown had been an itinerant doctor and could give particular accounts of towns in England and countries in Europe.
Dr. Brown was much of an unbeliever.
Dr. Brown travestied the Bible in doggrel verse, similar to Cotton's treatment of Virgil.
Franklin stopped at a poor inn at noon on his journey and wished he had never left home.
Franklin was suspected of being a runaway servant during his travels.
Franklin reached an inn kept by Dr. Brown within eight or ten miles of Burlington.
Franklin found Dr. Brown to be sociable and friendly after learning that Franklin had read a little.
Dr. Brown was much of an unbeliever and attempted to travesty the Bible in doggrel verse.
Franklin arrived in Burlington on a Saturday and found the regular boats had already left.
Franklin was invited to lodge with an old woman in Burlington who sold gingerbread.
The old woman understood Franklin was a printer and wanted him to stay in Burlington.
The old woman gave Franklin a dinner of ox-cheek and accepted only a pot of ale in return.
Franklin found a boat going toward Philadelphia on Saturday evening and was taken aboard.
The boat rowed all the way to Philadelphia because there was no wind.
Franklin and the boat company landed near Cooper's Creek around midnight in October.
Franklin arrived in Philadelphia on Sunday morning around eight or nine o'clock at the Market-street wharf.
Franklin was wearing his working dress when he arrived in Philadelphia, with his best clothes coming by sea.
Franklin's pockets were stuffed with shirts and stockings when he arrived in Philadelphia.
Franklin had only a Dutch dollar and about a shilling in copper when he arrived in Philadelphia.
Franklin gave the boat people a shilling in copper for his passage, which they initially refused.
Franklin believed a man is sometimes more generous when he has little money than when he has plenty.
Franklin met a boy with bread near the market-house in Philadelphia.
Franklin asked for bisket at a baker's in Second-street, intending the kind made in Boston.
Franklin asked for a three-penny loaf and received three great puffy rolls instead.
Franklin walked up Market-street eating rolls, passing by the door of Mr. Read, his future wife's father.
Miss Read saw Franklin eating a roll in the street and thought he made an awkward, ridiculous appearance.
Franklin gave two of his rolls to a woman and her child from the boat.
Franklin followed clean-dressed people into the great meeting-house of the Quakers near the market.
Franklin fell asleep during the Quaker meeting due to drowsiness from labor and lack of rest.
The Quaker meeting-house was the first house Franklin slept in during his time in Philadelphia.
Franklin met a young Quaker man who directed him to the Crooked Billet in Water-street for lodging.
Franklin was suspected of being a runaway at the Crooked Billet due to his youth and appearance.
Franklin slept until six in the evening at the Crooked Billet, then was called to supper.
Franklin visited Andrew Bradford the printer and found Bradford's father in the shop.
Andrew Bradford told Franklin he did not currently want a hand but offered him lodging and occasional work.
Andrew Bradford directed Franklin to Keimer, another printer in town who might employ him.
Keimer tested Franklin's printing skills by putting a composing stick in his hand.
Franklin observed that Andrew Bradford was a crafty old sophister and Keimer was a mere novice.
Keimer's printing-house had an old shattered press and one small, worn-out font of English.
Keimer was composing an Elegy on Aquilla Rose when Franklin arrived.
Franklin endeavored to put Keimer's press into order.
Franklin returned to Bradford's house for lodging and work.
Keimer sent for Franklin a few days later to print off the Elegy.
Franklin found that Andrew Bradford had not been bred to printing and was very illiterate.
Franklin found that Keimer was a mere compositor who knew nothing of presswork.
Keimer had been one of the French prophets and could act their enthusiastic agitations.
Keimer did not profess any particular religion but something of all on occasion.
Keimer was very ignorant of the world and had a good deal of the knave in his composition.
Keimer got Franklin lodging at Mr. Read's house.
Franklin made a more respectable appearance to Miss Read after his chest and clothes arrived.
Franklin began to have acquaintance among young people of Philadelphia who were lovers of reading.
Franklin spent his evenings pleasantly with young people who loved reading.
Franklin gained money by his industry and frugality.
Franklin lived agreeably in Philadelphia and tried to forget Boston.
Franklin did not want anyone in Boston to know where he resided, except his friend Collins.
Franklin's brother-in-law Robert Holmes was master of a sloop that traded between Boston and Delaware.
Robert Holmes heard of Franklin in Newcastle and wrote him a letter about his friends' concern in Boston.
Franklin wrote to Robert Holmes explaining his reasons for quitting Boston.
Sir William Keith, governor of the province, was at Newcastle when Holmes received Franklin's letter.
Sir William Keith read Franklin's letter and seemed surprised at Franklin's age.
Sir William Keith said Franklin appeared a young man of promising parts and should be encouraged.
Sir William Keith said the printers at Philadelphia were wretched ones.
Sir William Keith offered to procure public business for Franklin if he would set up in Philadelphia.
Sir William Keith came to Keimer's printing-house to visit Franklin.
Keimer was surprised when the governor inquired for Franklin instead of him.
Sir William Keith invited Franklin to a tavern at the corner of Third-street to taste Madeira.
Sir William Keith and Colonel French proposed that Franklin set up his own business.
Sir William Keith offered to give Franklin a letter to his father recommending the project.
Sir William Keith sent for Franklin to dine with him several times.
Franklin considered dining with the governor a very great honor.
Franklin took leave of Keimer in late April 1724, saying he was going to see his friends.
The governor gave Franklin an ample letter with flattering things about him to his father.
Franklin's vessel struck on a shoal going down the bay and sprung a leak.
Franklin took his turn pumping water during the voyage to Boston.
Franklin arrived safely in Boston after about a fortnight at sea.
Franklin had been absent from Boston for seven months.
Franklin's family was surprised and glad to see him, except his brother.
Franklin was better dressed than ever before, with a genteel new suit, a watch, and nearly five pounds sterling in silver.
Franklin's brother received him not very frankly and turned to his work again.
Franklin praised Philadelphia and the happy life he led there to the journeymen.
Franklin expressed his strong intention of returning to Philadelphia.
Franklin showed the journeymen a handful of silver, which was unusual in Boston where paper was the money.
Franklin showed the journeymen his watch.
Franklin gave the journeymen a piece of eight to drink.
Franklin's visit offended his brother extremely.
Franklin's mother wished to see Franklin and his brother reconciled and living as brothers.
Franklin returned to Philadelphia dressed in a new suit, with a watch, and about five pounds sterling in silver.
Franklin's brother James received him coldly and turned back to his work.
Franklin showed the journeymen silver money, which was unusual in Boston where paper money was standard.
Franklin's visit offended his brother James so much that James said he could never forgive the insult.
Governor Keith wrote a letter to Franklin's father proposing to set Franklin up in the printing business.
Franklin's father thought Keith lacked discretion for proposing to set up a boy who was three years away from being of legal age.
Franklin's friend Collins was a clerk in the post-office.
Collins was pleased with Franklin's account of Pennsylvania and determined to go there.
Collins left his collection of mathematics and natural philosophy books to come with Franklin's to New York.
Franklin's father advised him to behave respectfully, obtain general esteem, and avoid lampooning and libeling.
Franklin's father told him that by steady industry and prudent parsimony he might save enough by age twenty-one to set up in business.
Franklin's father promised to help him with the rest if he came near the amount needed.
Franklin's father gave him small gifts as tokens of his and Franklin's mother's love.
Franklin visited his brother John in Newport, Rhode Island, who had been married and settled there.
Franklin's brother John received him very affectionately and always loved him.
A friend of John's named Vernon had about thirty-five pounds currency due to him in Pennsylvania and asked Franklin to collect it.
A Quaker woman on the ship to New York warned Franklin about two young women who were traveling as companions.
The Quaker woman's warning proved correct when the two women were discovered to be thieves and had stolen items from the ship's captain.
Franklin's friend Collins had arrived in New York before him.
Franklin and Collins had been intimate from childhood and read the same books together.
Collins had a wonderful genius for mathematical learning and far surpassed Franklin in this skill.
While Franklin was away, Collins acquired a habit of drinking brandy and was drunk every day since arriving in New York.
Collins had gambled and lost his money, forcing Franklin to pay his lodgings and expenses to Philadelphia.
Governor Burnet of New York heard from the ship's captain that Franklin had many books and invited him to visit.
Governor Burnet showed Franklin his large library and they had a good conversation about books and authors.
This was the second governor who took notice of Franklin, which pleased him greatly.
Franklin received Vernon's money on the way to Philadelphia, which was necessary to finish the journey.
Collins wished to be employed in a counting-house but was unsuccessful in his applications.
Collins continued to borrow money from Franklin, promising repayment when he found work.
Franklin and Collins quarreled about Collins's drinking.
In a boat on the Delaware, Collins refused to row and Franklin threw him overboard after Collins struck at him.
Franklin knew Collins was a good swimmer and was not concerned about him.
Franklin and Collins hardly exchanged a civil word after the boat incident.
A West India captain agreed to take Collins to Barbados as a tutor for a gentleman's sons.
Collins promised to remit Franklin money to discharge his debt but Franklin never heard from him again.
Franklin considered the breaking into Vernon's money one of the first great errors of his life.
Governor Keith told Franklin he would set him up in the printing business himself.
Franklin presented Governor Keith with an inventory of a small print-house amounting to about one hundred pounds sterling.
Governor Keith suggested Franklin go to England to choose the types and establish correspondences in the bookselling and stationery trade.
Franklin agreed to go to England on the annual ship called Annis.
Franklin continued working with Keimer while waiting for the Annis to sail.
Franklin was in daily apprehension of being called upon by Vernon to remit the money.
On his first voyage from Boston, Franklin was becalmed off Block Island.
Franklin had stuck to a resolution of not eating animal food, following his master Tryon's teachings.
Franklin considered taking fish as a kind of unprovoked murder since they could not injure humans.
When Franklin saw smaller fish in the stomachs of caught fish, he reasoned that if fish eat each other, humans could eat them.
Franklin returned to eating animal food and continued to eat with other people, only occasionally returning to a vegetable diet.
Franklin and Keimer lived on a pretty good familiar footing and agreed tolerably well.
Keimer suspected nothing of Franklin's plan to set up his own business.
Keimer retained great enthusiasm and loved argumentation.
Franklin used the Socratic method to work Keimer into difficulties and contradictions during their disputations.
Keimer grew so cautious from Franklin's questioning that he would ask what Franklin intended to infer before answering common questions.
Keimer proposed that Franklin be his colleague in setting up a new sect, with Keimer preaching and Franklin confuting opponents.
Keimer wore his beard at full length because of the Mosaic law against marring the corners of the beard.
Keimer kept the Seventh day Sabbath as an essential practice.
Franklin disliked both Keimer's beard and Sabbath practices but agreed to admit them if Keimer would adopt a no-animal-food diet.
Keimer doubted his constitution could bear a vegetarian diet.
Franklin and Keimer held a vegetarian diet for three months.
A woman in the neighborhood prepared forty different vegetarian dishes for Franklin and Keimer at eighteenpence sterling per week each.
Franklin found the vegetarian diet convenient because of its cheapness.
Keimer suffered greatly on the vegetarian diet and eventually ordered a roast pig.
Keimer ate the entire roast pig before Franklin and two women friends arrived for dinner.
Franklin had made courtship to Miss Read and had great respect and affection for her.
Franklin had reason to believe Miss Read had the same feelings for him.
Miss Read's mother thought it prudent to prevent Franklin and Miss Read from going too far since Franklin was about to take a long voyage.
Franklin and Miss Read were both only a little above eighteen years old.
Franklin's chief acquaintances at this time were Charles Osborne, Joseph Watson, and James Ralph, all lovers of reading.
Franklin made changes to his life by easy gradations.
Keimer was tired of the vegetarian project and ordered a roast pig.
Franklin made courtship to Miss Read during this time.
Franklin had great respect and affection for Miss Read.
Franklin and Miss Read were both a little above eighteen years old.
Miss Read's mother thought it prudent to prevent Franklin and Miss Read from going too far before his voyage.
Franklin expected to be set up in his business after his return.
Franklin's chief acquaintances were Charles Osborne, Joseph Watson, and James Ralph, all lovers of reading.
Watson was a pious, sensible young man of great integrity.
Ralph and Collins had been unsettled by Franklin in their religious principles.
Osborne was sensible, candid, frank, sincere and affectionate to his friends.
Osborne was too fond of criticizing in literary matters.
Ralph was ingenious, genteel in his manners, and extremely eloquent.
Franklin never knew a prettier talker than Ralph.
Franklin and his friends took pleasant walks together on Sundays into the woods near Schuylkill.
Ralph was inclined to pursue the study of poetry.
Franklin approved of amusing oneself with poetry to improve one's language, but no farther.
Franklin and his friends agreed to each produce a version of the eighteenth Psalm.
Franklin transcribed Ralph's poem to make it appear as his own.
Franklin defended his text when criticized.
Osborne praised Franklin's writing ability, noting he seemed to have no choice of words in conversation but wrote with great force and fire.
Ralph discovered the trick Franklin and Osborne had played on him.
Franklin did all he could to dissuade Ralph from becoming a poet.
Ralph continued scribbling verses until Pope cured him.
Ralph became a pretty good prose writer.
Watson died in Franklin's arms a few years after, much lamented.
Osborne went to the West Indies and became an eminent lawyer.
Franklin and Osborne made a serious agreement that whoever died first would visit the other from the afterlife.
Osborne never fulfilled his promise to visit Franklin after death.
The governor frequently had Franklin to his house.
The governor promised to set Franklin up in business.
Franklin was appointed to call at different times to receive letters of recommendation from the governor.
The governor kept postponing giving Franklin the letters.
Ralph was married and had one child when he decided to accompany Franklin to London.
Ralph intended to leave his wife and never return to Philadelphia.
Franklin left Philadelphia in a ship that anchored at Newcastle.
The governor did not give Franklin the promised letters before he sailed.
Andrew Hamilton, a famous Philadelphia lawyer, took passage on the same ship.
Franklin and Ralph were forced to take a berth in the steerage initially.
Colonel French showed Franklin great respect on board the ship.
Franklin and Ralph were invited into the cabin by the other gentlemen.
Mr. Denham contracted a friendship with Franklin that continued during his life.
The voyage to England was not pleasant due to bad weather.
Franklin arrived in London on December 24, 1724.
The letters Franklin thought were from Governor Keith were actually from Riddlesden.
The stationer told Franklin that Riddlesden was a complete rascal.
Franklin began to doubt Governor Keith's sincerity.
Denham told Franklin that Keith had no credit to give and was not dependable.
Riddlesden had half ruined Miss Read's father by persuading him to be bound for him.
Keith was concerned in a secret scheme with Riddlesden to the prejudice of Hamilton.
Franklin warned Hamilton about the scheme against him.
Hamilton thanked Franklin cordially for the information.
Hamilton became Franklin's friend, greatly to his advantage afterwards.
Governor Keith had a habit of wishing to please everybody and giving expectations instead of actual help.
Keith was an ingenious, sensible man and a pretty good writer.
Keith was a good governor for the people but not for the proprietaries.
Franklin and Ralph took lodgings together in Little Britain at three shillings and sixpence a week.
Ralph found some poor relations in London who were unable to assist him.
Ralph had no money left after paying his passage to London.
Franklin had fifteen pistoles when he arrived in London.
Ralph borrowed money from Franklin to subsist while looking for work.
Ralph tried to get employment as an actor but was advised against it by Wilkes.
Ralph proposed to Roberts to write a weekly paper like the Spectator.
Ralph tried to get employment as a hackney writer but could find no vacancy.
Franklin immediately got work at Palmer's printing-house in Bartholomew Close.
Franklin continued at Palmer's for near a year.
Franklin spent a good deal of his earnings with Ralph on plays and other amusements.
Franklin and Ralph consumed all of Franklin's pistoles.
Franklin was constantly kept unable to pay his passage due to expenses.
Ralph seemed to forget his wife and child.
Franklin forgot his engagements with Miss Read by degrees.
Franklin wrote only one letter to Miss Read, letting her know he was not likely to return soon.
Franklin considered his neglect of Miss Read one of the great errata of his life.
Franklin was employed at Palmer's composing for the second edition of Wollaston's Religion of Nature.
Franklin wrote a metaphysical piece called A Dissertation on Liberty and Necessity, Pleasure and Pain.
Franklin inscribed his pamphlet to Ralph.
Franklin printed a small number of his pamphlet.
Palmer seriously expostulated with Franklin about the principles of his pamphlet, which appeared abominable to him.
Franklin considered printing his pamphlet another erratum.
Franklin made an acquaintance with Wilcox, a bookseller in Little Britain.
Wilcox had an immense collection of second-hand books.
Franklin arranged with Wilcox to take, read, and return any of his books on reasonable terms.
Franklin's pamphlet fell into the hands of Lyons, a surgeon.
Lyons took great notice of Franklin and called on him often.
Lyons introduced Franklin to Dr. Mandeville, author of the Fable of the Bees.
Mandeville had a club at the Horns alehouse and was the soul of it.
Lyons introduced Franklin to Dr. Pemberton at Batson's Coffee-house.
Pemberton promised to give Franklin an opportunity to see Sir Isaac Newton.
Franklin was extremely desirous of seeing Sir Isaac Newton.
Franklin never got the opportunity to see Sir Isaac Newton.
Franklin brought over a purse made of asbestos that purifies by fire.
Sir Hans Sloane heard of Franklin's asbestos purse and came to see him.
Sloane invited Franklin to his house in Bloomsbury Square.
Sloane paid Franklin handsomely for the asbestos purse.
A young milliner lodged in Franklin's house.
The milliner was genteelly bred, sensible, lively, and of pleasing conversation.
Franklin wrote an essay entitled 'The Infallibility of Human Judgment' that led to an acquaintance with a man who took great notice of him.
Franklin was introduced to Dr. Mandeville, author of 'Fable of the Bees,' at the Horns alehouse in Cheapside.
Franklin was introduced to Dr. Pemberton at Batson's Coffee-house, who promised to arrange for Franklin to meet Sir Isaac Newton.
Franklin was extremely desirous of meeting Sir Isaac Newton, but this never happened.
Franklin brought an asbestos purse from America that could be purified by fire.
Sir Hans Sloane heard of Franklin's asbestos purse, visited him, and paid him handsomely for it.
Franklin lodged in a house where a young milliner lived who was sensible, lively, and had pleasing conversation.
Franklin's friend Ralph read plays to the milliner in the evenings, and they grew intimate.
Ralph left London to take a country school position in Berkshire, teaching reading and writing to ten or a dozen boys at sixpence per week each.
Ralph assumed Franklin's name without permission.
Franklin attempted familiarities with Mrs. T---- and she repulsed him with proper resentment.
Franklin's breach with Ralph resulted from his inappropriate behavior toward Mrs. T----.
Franklin lent Ralph money that he never expected to be repaid.
Franklin left Palmer's printing house to work at Watts's printing house near Lincoln's Inn Fields.
Franklin drank only water while other workmen at Watts's drank beer.
Franklin was called the 'Water-American' by his coworkers because he was stronger than beer drinkers despite drinking only water.
Franklin carried large forms of types in both hands while others carried only one form in both hands.
Franklin thought the custom of drinking strong beer to gain strength was detestable.
Franklin argued that bread and water would provide more strength than beer because bread contained more flour.
Franklin was demanded to pay five shillings as a 'bien venu' fee when moving to the composing-room.
Franklin refused to pay the five shillings fee and was treated as an excommunicate for two or three weeks.
Franklin's coworkers mixed his sorts, transposed his pages, and broke his matter as mischief.
Franklin eventually paid the five shillings fee to avoid continued ill treatment.
Franklin proposed reasonable alterations to the chapel laws and carried them against all opposition.
Franklin convinced many coworkers to switch from beer and bread breakfast to hot water-gruel with pepper, bread crumbs, and butter for three half-pence.
Franklin watched the pay-table on Saturday nights and collected money owed by coworkers for their beer.
Franklin sometimes paid nearly thirty shillings a week on his coworkers' beer accounts.
Franklin was esteemed as a good 'riggite,' meaning a jocular verbal satirist.
Franklin never took a St. Monday holiday.
Franklin's constant attendance and quickness at composing led to him being put on all work of dispatch, which was better paid.
Franklin lodged in Little Britain initially, then moved to Duke-street opposite the Romish Chapel.
Franklin paid three shillings and sixpence per week for lodging at the Italian warehouse.
Franklin's landlady was a widow, elderly, and had been converted to Catholicism by her husband.
Franklin's landlady had lived among people of distinction and knew many anecdotes from the time of Charles the Second.
Franklin's landlady was lame in her knees with gout.
Franklin spent evenings with his landlady for her entertaining conversation.
Franklin and his landlady had supper of only half an anchovy each on a strip of bread and butter with half a pint of ale.
Franklin kept good hours and gave little trouble in his landlady's family.
Franklin's landlady reduced his rent from three shillings and sixpence to one shilling and sixpence per week.
A seventy-year-old maiden lady lived in a garret of Franklin's landlady's house and lived as a nun without being in a nunnery.
The maiden lady had given all her estate to charitable uses, reserving only twelve pounds a year to live on.
The maiden lady lived on water-gruel only and used no fire except to boil it.
The maiden lady had lived in the garret for many years rent-free, permitted by successive Catholic tenants.
A priest visited the maiden lady daily to hear her confession.
Franklin contracted an acquaintance with Wygate, an ingenious young man at Watts's printing-house.
Wygate was better educated than most printers, was a tolerable Latinist, spoke French, and loved reading.
Franklin taught Wygate and a friend to swim at twice going into the river, and they soon became good swimmers.
Franklin swam from near Chelsea to Blackfriars, performing many feats of activity in the water.
Franklin had been delighted with swimming since childhood.
Franklin studied and practiced all of Thevenot's swimming motions and positions and added some of his own.
Franklin aimed at graceful and easy swimming as well as useful swimming.
Wygate proposed to Franklin that they travel all over Europe together, supporting themselves by working at printing.
Franklin was once inclined to accept Wygate's proposal to travel Europe together.
Franklin's friend Mr. Denham dissuaded him from traveling Europe and advised him to return to Pennsylvania.
Mr. Denham had formerly been in business at Bristol but failed in debt.
Mr. Denham compounded with his creditors and went to America.
Mr. Denham acquired a plentiful fortune in America through close application to business as a merchant.
Mr. Denham returned to England in the same ship as Franklin.
Mr. Denham invited his old creditors to an entertainment and thanked them for the easy composition they favored him with.
Mr. Denham gave each of his old creditors an order on a banker for the full amount of unpaid remainder with interest.
Mr. Denham proposed to take Franklin to Philadelphia as his clerk to keep his books.
Mr. Denham offered to instruct Franklin in bookkeeping and have him copy his letters and attend the store.
Mr. Denham promised to promote Franklin by sending him with cargo to the West Indies and procuring him commissions.
Mr. Denham offered to establish Franklin handsomely if he managed well.
Franklin agreed to work for Mr. Denham at fifty pounds a year in Pennsylvania money.
Franklin was grown tired of London.
Franklin remembered with pleasure the happy months he had spent in Pennsylvania.
Franklin left printing, as he thought, forever to work for Mr. Denham.
Franklin was employed going about with Mr. Denham among tradesmen to purchase various articles.
Sir William Wyndham sent for Franklin after hearing of his swimming from Chelsea to Blackfriars.
Sir William Wyndham wished Franklin to teach his two sons to swim.
Franklin thought that if he had remained in England and opened a swimming-school, he might have gotten a good deal of money.
Franklin thought that if Sir William Wyndham's offer had come sooner, he probably would not have returned to America so soon.
Franklin spent about eighteen months in London.
Franklin worked hard at his business during most of his time in London.
Franklin spent little money on himself except for seeing plays and buying books.
Franklin's friend Ralph owed him about twenty-seven pounds.
Franklin taught Wygate and another young man to swim in a few hours.
Sir William Wyndham proposed to pay Franklin handsomely to teach his two sons to swim.
Franklin considered opening a swimming-school in England if the opportunity had come sooner.
Franklin worked hard at his business in London and spent little on himself except for plays and books.
Franklin's friend Ralph owed him about twenty-seven pounds that he was never likely to receive.
Franklin loved Ralph despite the debt because Ralph had many amiable qualities.
Franklin picked up some very ingenious acquaintances in London whose conversation was of great advantage to him.
Franklin read considerably during his time in London.
Franklin sailed from Gravesend on July 23, 1726.
Franklin landed in Philadelphia on October 11, 1726.
Keith was no longer governor when Franklin returned to Philadelphia, having been superseded by Major Gordon.
Miss Read married a potter named Rogers in Franklin's absence after her friends persuaded her he would not return.
Miss Read was never happy with Rogers and soon parted from him.
Rogers had another wife, making his marriage to Miss Read invalid.
Rogers was a worthless fellow but an excellent workman.
Rogers got into debt, ran away in 1727 or 1728, and died in the West Indies.
Mr. Denham took a store on Water Street where Franklin worked.
Franklin attended Denham's business diligently, studied accounts, and became expert at selling.
Franklin lodged and boarded with Mr. Denham, who counseled him as a father.
Franklin respected and loved Mr. Denham.
In early February 1726/7, when Franklin had just passed his twenty-first year, both he and Denham became ill.
Franklin suffered from pleurisy, which nearly killed him.
Franklin gave up hope during his pleurisy and was disappointed when he recovered.
Franklin regretted recovering because he would have to do disagreeable work over again.
Mr. Denham's illness carried him off.
Denham left Franklin a small legacy in a nuncupative will as a token of his kindness.
Franklin's brother-in-law Holmes advised him to return to his printing business.
Keimer tempted Franklin with an offer of large wages to manage his printing-house.
Franklin had heard a bad character of Keimer from Keimer's wife and her friends in London.
Hugh Meredith was a Welsh Pennsylvanian, thirty years old, honest and sensible, but given to drink.
Stephen Potts was a young countryman of uncommon natural parts and great wit and humor, but a little idle.
Keimer agreed to raise Meredith and Potts's wages by a shilling every three months as they improved.
John, a wild Irishman, had his service for four years purchased by Keimer from a ship captain.
George Webb was an Oxford scholar whose time for four years Keimer had bought.
David Harry was a country boy whom Keimer had taken as an apprentice.
Franklin perceived that Keimer's intention was to have him train the cheap hands so Keimer could do without him.
Franklin put Keimer's printing-house in order and brought the hands to do better work.
George Webb was not more than eighteen years old.
Webb was born in Gloucester and educated at a grammar-school there.
Webb was distinguished among scholars for his performance in plays.
Webb belonged to the Witty Club in Gloucester.
Webb had written pieces in prose and verse printed in Gloucester newspapers.
Webb was sent to Oxford but stayed only about a year.
Webb wished to see London and become a player.
Webb received a quarterly allowance of fifteen guineas from Oxford.
Webb hid his gown in a furze bush and walked to London instead of paying his debts.
Webb fell into bad company in London and spent his guineas.
Webb could not be introduced among the players and grew necessitous.
Webb pawned his clothes and wanted bread in London.
Webb signed indentures with a crimp and came to America.
Webb never wrote to his friends about what became of him.
Webb was lively, witty, good-natured, and a pleasant companion, but idle, thoughtless, and imprudent.
John the Irishman soon ran away from Keimer's printing-house.
Franklin lived very agreeably with the other hands who respected him more as they found Keimer incapable of instructing them.
Keimer did not work on Saturday, which was his Sabbath, giving Franklin two days for reading.
Franklin's acquaintance with ingenious people in Philadelphia increased.
Keimer treated Franklin with great civility and apparent regard.
Franklin was uneasy about his debt to Vernon, which he was unable to pay.
Franklin was a poor economist at this time.
Vernon kindly made no demand for repayment of his debt.
The printing-house often wanted sorts and there was no letter-founder in America.
Franklin had seen types cast at James's in London but without much attention to the manner.
Franklin contrived a mould and made use of the letters as puncheons to strike matrices in lead.
Franklin supplied deficiencies in types in a tolerable way.
Franklin engraved several things on occasion.
Franklin made the ink for the printing-house.
Franklin was warehouseman and a factotum in the printing-house.
Franklin's services became less important as the other hands improved in the business.
When Keimer paid Franklin's second quarter's wages, he said they were too heavy and asked for an abatement.
Keimer grew less civil, put on more of the master, frequently found fault, and was captious.
Franklin went on with patience, thinking Keimer's encumbered circumstances were partly the cause.
A noise near the court-house caused Franklin to put his head out the window.
Keimer called out to Franklin in a loud voice and angry tone to mind his business.
Keimer's reproachful words were public, with neighbors as witnesses.
Keimer came into the printing-house and continued the quarrel with high words on both sides.
Keimer gave Franklin a quarter's warning, expressing a wish he had not been obliged to give such long warning.
Franklin told Keimer his wish was unnecessary and left instantly.
Franklin asked Meredith to take care of things he left and bring them to his lodgings.
Meredith came to Franklin's lodgings in the evening to talk over his affair.
Meredith had conceived a great regard for Franklin.
Meredith was unwilling for Franklin to leave while he remained in Keimer's house.
Meredith dissuaded Franklin from returning to his native country.
Meredith told Franklin that Keimer was in debt for all he possessed.
Meredith said Keimer's creditors began to be uneasy.
Meredith said Keimer kept his shop miserably and sold often without profit.
Meredith said Keimer trusted without keeping accounts.
Meredith predicted Keimer must fail, which would make a vacancy Franklin might profit from.
Franklin objected that he lacked money.
Meredith's father had a high opinion of Franklin.
Meredith's father was willing to advance money to set up a partnership with Franklin.
Meredith's time with Keimer would be out in the spring.
Meredith and Franklin expected their press and types to arrive from London by spring.
Meredith acknowledged he was no workman.
Franklin's skill in the business was to be set against the stock Meredith's father would furnish.
Meredith and Franklin agreed to share the profits equally.
Meredith's father approved of the partnership.
Meredith's father hoped Franklin would help his son abstain from dram-drinking.
Franklin gave an inventory to Meredith's father, who carried it to a merchant.
The secret of the partnership was to be kept until the things arrived.
Franklin was to get work at the other printing-house while waiting.
Franklin found no vacancy at the other printing-house and remained idle a few days.
Keimer expected to be employed to print paper money in New Jersey.
Keimer feared Bradford might engage Franklin and get the job from him.
Keimer sent Franklin a civil message saying old friends should not part for a few words.
Meredith persuaded Franklin to return to Keimer to give Meredith more opportunity for improvement.
Franklin and Keimer went on more smoothly after Franklin's return.
Keimer obtained the New Jersey job to print paper money.
Franklin contrived a copperplate press for the New Jersey job, the first seen in the country.
Franklin cut several ornaments and checks for the bills.
Franklin and Keimer went together to Burlington.
Franklin executed the New Jersey job to satisfaction.
Keimer received so large a sum for the work that he could keep his head above water much longer.
Franklin made an acquaintance with many principal people of the province at Burlington.
Several principal people of the province were appointed by the Assembly as a committee to attend the press.
The committee ensured no more bills were printed than the law directed.
The committee members brought friends with them for company.
Franklin's mind had been much more improved by reading than Keimer's.
Franklin's conversation seemed to be more valued than Keimer's.
The committee members had Franklin to their houses and introduced him to their friends.
The committee members showed Franklin much civility.
Keimer, though the master, was a little neglected by the committee members.
Keimer was an odd fish, ignorant of common life, and fond of rudely opposing received opinions.
Keimer was slovenly to extreme dirtiness.
Keimer was enthusiastic in some points of religion.
Keimer was a little knavish.
Franklin and Keimer continued at Burlington near three months.
Franklin acquired friends including Judge Allen, Samuel Bustill, Isaac Pearson, Joseph Cooper, and several Smiths.
Isaac Decow was the surveyor-general and a shrewd, sagacious old man.
Decow began for himself when young by wheeling clay for brick-makers.
Decow learned to write after he was of age.
Decow carried the chain for surveyors who taught him surveying.
Decow acquired a good estate by his industry.
Decow told Franklin he foresaw Franklin would work Keimer out of his business and make a fortune in Philadelphia.
Decow had no intimation of Franklin's intention to set up in Philadelphia.
Franklin's friends were afterwards of great use to him.
Franklin was occasionally of great use to some of his friends.
Franklin's friends continued their regard for him as long as they lived.
Franklin's parents had given him religious impressions early.
Franklin's parents brought him through childhood piously in the Dissenting way.
Franklin was scarce fifteen when he began to doubt Revelation itself.
Franklin doubted several points as he found them disputed in different books he read.
Books against Deism fell into Franklin's hands.
The books against Deism were said to be the substance of sermons preached at Boyle's Lectures.
The arguments of the Deists quoted in the books appeared stronger to Franklin than the refutations.
Franklin soon became a thorough Deist.
Franklin's arguments perverted Collins and Ralph to Deism.
Collins and Ralph afterwards wronged Franklin greatly without the least compunction.
Franklin recollected Keith's conduct towards him as a free-thinker.
Franklin recollected his own conduct towards Vernon and Miss Read, which gave him great trouble.
Franklin began to suspect that Deism, though it might be true, was not very useful.
Franklin wrote a London pamphlet with a motto from Dryden's lines.
Franklin's pamphlet concluded that nothing could possibly be wrong in the world.
Franklin's pamphlet argued that vice and virtue were empty distinctions.
Franklin later doubted whether his pamphlet was as clever as he once thought.
Franklin suspected an error had insinuated itself into his argument in the pamphlet.
Franklin believed that truth, sincerity, and integrity in dealings between people were of the utmost importance to happiness in life.
Franklin formed written resolutions to practice truth, sincerity, and integrity while he lived.
Franklin believed that certain actions might be forbidden because they were bad for people, or commanded because they were beneficial, in their own natures.
Franklin valued his character properly and determined to preserve it.
Franklin and his partner settled with Keimer and left him by his consent.
Franklin rented a house near the market for twenty-four pounds a year.
Franklin took in Thomas Godfrey, a glazier, and his family as lodgers to help pay rent.
Franklin's first customer brought him five shillings, which gave him more pleasure than any crown he earned later.
Franklin felt gratitude toward George House for bringing him his first customer, which made him more ready to assist young beginners.
Franklin formed a club called the Junto in the autumn of the preceding year with ingenious acquaintances.
Franklin met with the Junto on Friday evenings.
Franklin drew up rules requiring each Junto member to produce queries on Morals, Politics, or Natural Philosophy.
Franklin required Junto members to produce and read essays once every three months.
Franklin prohibited direct contradiction and expressions of positiveness in Junto opinions under small pecuniary penalties.
Joseph Breintnal was a copyer of deeds, a lover of poetry, and a member of the Junto.
Thomas Godfrey was a self-taught mathematician and inventor of Hadley's Quadrant.
Thomas Godfrey expected universal precision in everything and was not a pleasing companion.
Nicholas Scull was a surveyor who later became surveyor-general.
William Parsons was bred a shoemaker but acquired considerable knowledge of mathematics.
William Maugridge was a joiner and exquisite mechanic.
Robert Grace was a young gentleman of fortune, generous, lively, and witty.
William Coleman had the coolest, clearest head, the best heart, and the exactest morals of almost any man Franklin met.
Franklin's friendship with William Coleman continued without interruption for upwards of forty years.
The Junto was the best school of philosophy, morality, and politics that existed in the province.
Franklin composed one sheet a day of the Quaker history folio.
Hugh Meredith worked the press while Franklin composed the folio.
Franklin worked until eleven at night or later distributing type for the next day's work.
When one of Franklin's forms was broken and two pages reduced to pi, he immediately redistributed and composed it over again before bed.
Dr. Baird observed that Franklin's industry was superior to anything he had ever seen.
Franklin told George Webb in secret that he intended to begin a newspaper.
George Webb told Keimer about Franklin's newspaper plans.
Franklin wrote several pieces of entertainment for Bradford's paper under the title of the Busy Body.
Franklin burlesqued and ridiculed Keimer's newspaper proposals.
Keimer carried on his newspaper for three quarters of a year with at most only ninety subscribers.
Keimer offered his newspaper to Franklin for a trifle.
Franklin's first papers had better type and better printing than any before in the province.
Franklin wrote spirited remarks on the dispute between Governor Burnet and the Massachusetts Assembly.
Franklin's newspaper and its manager became much talked of within a few weeks.
The leading men of the province became subscribers to Franklin's newspaper.
Bradford had printed an address of the House to the governor in a coarse, blundering manner.
Franklin reprinted the House address elegantly and correctly and sent one to every member.
The House voted Franklin and his partner their printers for the year ensuing.
Mr. Hamilton was returned from England and had a seat in the House.
Mr. Hamilton interested himself strongly for Franklin and continued his patronage until his death.
Mr. Vernon put Franklin in mind of a debt he owed him but did not press him.
Franklin wrote Vernon an ingenuous letter of acknowledgment and asked for forbearance.
Franklin paid Vernon the principal with interest and many thanks.
Mr. Meredith's father was able to advance only one hundred pounds currency instead of the full amount expected.
A merchant sued Franklin and his partners for the remaining hundred pounds owed.
William Coleman and Robert Grace each offered separately to advance Franklin all the money necessary to take the whole business upon himself.
Coleman and Grace said Meredith was often seen drunk in the streets and playing at low games in alehouses.
Franklin told Coleman and Grace he could not propose separation while any prospect remained of the Merediths fulfilling their agreement.
Franklin proposed to Meredith that perhaps his father was unwilling to advance money for both of them.
Meredith said his father was really unable to advance more money.
Meredith said he was bred a farmer and it was folly to come to town at thirty years of age to learn a new trade.
Meredith said many Welsh people were going to settle in North Carolina where land was cheap.
Meredith asked Franklin to take the company debts, return his father's hundred pounds, pay his personal debts, and give him thirty pounds and a new saddle.
Franklin agreed to Meredith's proposal and it was drawn up in writing, signed, and sealed immediately.
Meredith went to Carolina and sent Franklin two long letters the next year about the country, climate, soil, and husbandry.
Franklin printed Meredith's letters about Carolina in the papers.
Franklin was bred a farmer.
Franklin came to town at thirty years of age to apprentice himself to learn a new trade.
Franklin found the printing business was not fit for him at that time.
Many Welsh people were settling in North Carolina where land was cheap.
Franklin was inclined to go to North Carolina and follow his old employment as a farmer.
Franklin agreed to take on the debts of the company, return money to his partner's father, pay personal debts, and give his partner thirty pounds and a new saddle in exchange for the partnership.
Franklin's partner went to Carolina and sent him two long letters the next year about that country.
Franklin printed his partner's letters about Carolina in the papers.
Franklin took half of what each of two friends had offered him for capital.
Franklin paid off the company's debts and went on with the business in his own name around 1729.
Franklin was on the side of adding more paper money in discussions at the Junto.
Franklin believed the first small sum of paper money struck in 1723 had done much good by increasing trade, employment, and inhabitants in the province.
Franklin remembered seeing most houses in Walnut Street between Second and Front streets with bills saying 'To be let' when he first walked about Philadelphia eating his roll.
Franklin wrote and printed an anonymous pamphlet entitled 'The Nature and Necessity of a Paper Currency.'
Franklin's pamphlet was well received by common people but disliked by rich men.
Franklin's friends employed him in printing the money as a reward for his service.
Franklin obtained the printing of Newcastle paper money through his friend Hamilton.
Franklin obtained the printing of the laws and votes of the Newcastle government.
Franklin opened a little stationer's shop.
Franklin had blanks of all sorts in his shop, assisted by his friend Breintnal.
Franklin had paper, parchment, and chapmen's books in his shop.
Whitemash, a compositor Franklin had known in London, came to work with him constantly and diligently.
Franklin took an apprentice who was the son of Aquilla Rose.
Franklin began gradually to pay off the debt he was under for the printing-house.
Franklin dressed plainly to secure his credit and character as a tradesman.
Franklin was seen at no places of idle diversion.
Franklin never went out fishing or shooting.
Franklin sometimes brought home paper he purchased at stores through the streets on a wheelbarrow to show he was not above his business.
Franklin was esteem'd an industrious, thriving young man.
Keimer's credit and business declined daily and he was forced to sell his printing-house.
Keimer went to Barbadoes and lived there in very poor circumstances.
David Harry, whom Franklin had instructed, set up in Keimer's place at Philadelphia.
Franklin proposed a partnership to Harry, which Harry rejected with scorn.
Harry was very proud, dressed like a gentleman, lived expensively, took much diversion and pleasure abroad, and ran in debt.
Harry followed Keimer to Barbadoes, taking the printing-house with him.
Bradford was the old competitor with Franklin at Philadelphia.
Bradford was rich and easy and did little printing.
Bradford kept the post-office.
Franklin thought meanly of Bradford for forbidding him to send papers by the post, and resolved never to imitate it when he came into Bradford's situation.
Franklin had continued to board with Godfrey, who lived in part of his house with his wife and children.
Godfrey had one side of the shop for his glazier's business but worked little, being absorbed in his mathematics.
Mrs. Godfrey projected a match for Franklin with a relation's daughter.
Franklin expected as much money with the daughter as would pay off his remaining debt for the printing-house, which was about a hundred pounds.
The girl's family said they had no such sum to spare and suggested mortgaging their house.
The girl's family informed Franklin they did not approve the match after inquiring of Bradford about the printing business.
The girl's family said the types would soon be worn out and more would be wanted.
The girl's family said Keimer and Harry had failed one after the other and Franklin would probably soon follow them.
Franklin was forbidden the house and the daughter was shut up.
Franklin resented the treatment and went no more to the house.
Franklin declared absolutely his resolution to have nothing more to do with that family.
The Godfreys removed, leaving Franklin the whole house.
